title: practical english usage - Part 19
content:
•    The  old past participle drunken is used as an adjective in some expressions dive - dived/dove (/doʊ  v/) - dived •  Dive is regular in British English, but can be irregular in American: participles with -t are also quite common.

regular in American English. In British English, irregular past tenses and •  Burn, dream, kneel, lean, learn, smell, spell, spill and spoil are all generally /et/ for ate is substandard in AmE   (but a standard variant in BrE).

•    Note the standard AmE   pronunciations of ate (/eɪt/) and shone (/ʃoon/).

(= injure in a battle) wound /wu:nd/                 wounded                           wounded tighten a spring, etc) wind /waind/ (= turn,   wound /waond/                 wound /waond/ gently over) stroke (= pass the hand    stroked                            stroked strike (= hit)                     struck                              struck rise (= go/get up)              rose                                   risen raise (= put up)                 raised                              raised live                                     lived                                   lived leave                                 left                                   left For more details of these three verbs, ►    507.

lie (= say things that are  lied                                     lied lie (= be down)                  lay                                       lain lay (= put down flat)     laid                                    laid flee (= run away)               fled                                    fled fly (= move in the air)     flew                                     flown =  move) flow (of a liquid               flowed                                flowed institution) an organisation or found (= start up               founded                          founded something lost) find (= get back                   found                               found fill                                       filled                                 filled feel                                    felt                                    felt fall                                      fell                                         fallen Infinitive                   Simple past                    Past participle 2 verbs     that    are   easily     confused Section1 Verbs grammar •  2  active verb tenses example continuation, completion, present importance.

and perfect forms express ideas that are not simply concerned with time - for used to talk about the future (e.g. I'm seeing Daniel tomorrow). Also, progressive events (e.g. It would be better if we went home now). And present verbs can be Morocco last January), but also about unreal or uncertain present or future past verb like went is not only used to talk about past events (e.g.  We went to There is not a direct relationship between verb forms and time. For example, a 2 tenses     and   time and structures that indicate time.

importance. In Practical English Usage the term 'tense' is used for all verb forms questions of terminology, not grammatical fact, and have no practical 'aspect', and they say that English has 'no future tense’ . These are principally for forms like is going or has gone they prefer to talk about progressive or perfect Some grammarians use the word 'tense' only for simple forms like goes or went; present perfect: I have started present progressive: I am starting simple present: I start (have + past participle ►    6).

simple; progressive or continuous (be + -ing ►    3); and perfect For each kind of time, there are three possibilities with most verbs: past: Who said that?

future: She will see you tomorrow.

present: I’ m  watching you.

English verbs can refer to present, future or past time.

1  present,      future     and   past;   simple,     progressive         and   perfect 2 active     verb    tenses •  Sung and sunk are sometimes used instead of sang and sank.

•  Spit has both spit and spat as past tense and participle in American English.

•  Speed can also have regular forms, especially in the expression speeded up.
